coredata.dc:title: Polymer quantum dots doped in metal organic framework for sono-catalytic degradation of sulfamethazine
coredata.dc:creator: Mogharbel, Roaa T.
Alorabi, Ali Q.
Alkhamis, Kholood M.
Alrefaee, Salhah H.
Al-bonayan, Ameena M.
Al-Qahtani, Salhah D.
El-Metwaly, Nashwa M.
coredata.dc:description: Sulfamethazine as one of sulfa drugs could be discharged in the surface, drinking and ground water, to be accumulated in the organs, migrating later to foodstuff and eventually raises the possibility of developing a variety of infectious diseases in human beings and animals. On the other hand, polymer quantum dots (PQDs) as a type of carbon-based nanostructures are exhibited with excellent photoluminescent properties, abundancy, easy of surface decoration and biocompatibility with low toxic effects. Herein, simple and environmentally benign technique was reported to reuse acrylate waste fibers (AWF) for nucleation of PQDs (size = 5.6 nm, zetapotential = -6 to -11). The so-nucleated PQDs were successively doped within metal organic framework (namely, MIL-125-NH2) to prepare PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, to be applicable as sono-photocatalyst for degradation of sulfamethazine. Successive doping of PQDs within MIL-125-NH2 was approved via different instrumental analysis such as Electron Microscope, X-ray diffraction, and infrared spectroscopy. Kinetic parameters for sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine were also estimated. The recyclability of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite was also approved. PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as sono-photocatalyst exhibited the highest affinity for full degradation (99.9%) within 90 min of rate constant of 28.79 ± 1.21± 1.5 × 10−3 min−1. For PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, the percentage of sono-photocatalytic degradation for sulfamethazine was lowered from 99.9% to be 96% to be reduced with only 4% after recycling for 5 times. Reaction mechanisms for ingraining PQDs and the sono-photocatalytic action of the prepared of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite for degradation of sulfamethazine were elucidated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfamethazine as one of sulfa drugs could be discharged in the surface, drinking and ground water, to be accumulated in the organs, migrating later to foodstuff and eventually raises the possibility of developing a variety of infectious diseases in human beings and animals. On the other hand, polymer quantum dots (PQDs) as a type of carbon-based nanostructures are exhibited with excellent photoluminescent properties, abundancy, easy of surface decoration and biocompatibility with low toxic effects. Herein, simple and environmentally benign technique was reported to reuse acrylate waste fibers (AWF) for nucleation of PQDs (size = 5.6 nm, zetapotential = -6 to -11). The so-nucleated PQDs were successively doped within metal organic framework (namely, MIL-125-NH2) to prepare PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, to be applicable as sono-photocatalyst for degradation of sulfamethazine. Successive doping of PQDs within MIL-125-NH2 was approved via different instrumental analysis such as Electron Microscope, X-ray diffraction, and infrared spectroscopy. Kinetic parameters for sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine were also estimated. The recyclability of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite was also approved. PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as sono-photocatalyst exhibited the highest affinity for full degradation (99.9%) within 90 min of rate constant of 28.79 ± 1.21± 1.5 × 10−3 min−1. For PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, the percentage of sono-photocatalytic degradation for sulfamethazine was lowered from 99.9% to be 96% to be reduced with only 4% after recycling for 5 times. Reaction mechanisms for ingraining PQDs and the sono-photocatalytic action of the prepared of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite for degradation of sulfamethazine were elucidated.

ce:list-item.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are widely exploited and are interestingly considered owing to the rapid therapeutical affinity and protection benefits for both of human beings and animals. However, antibiotics also reported to be a type of risky environmental pollutants from different origins. The largest source of human pharmaceutical reagents is in the sewage, including not only the patients and hospitals wastewater but also pharmaceutical manufacturers wastewater [1]. In addition to, the agricultural resource of this type of environmental pollution causes also a hazard effects, as the most of animal feedings contain veterinary medicinal reagents that without being consumed, could logically pollute the surrounding environment [2]. Sulfonamides or sulfa drugs (SAs) are a vital category of antibiotics, are characterized as inexpensive drugs, protective/therapeutic agents against certain protozoa, Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, and fungi [3,4]. Thus, it is not surprisingly mentioned that the type of drugs is widely applicable as human and veterinary medicinal reagents. Numerous approaches focused on the effect of SAs on the environmental pollution, were looked in details at the possible risky effects of pollution and also at the possible methodologies for the removal of these type of veterinary and human antibiotics [5–7].

Sulfamethazine (SMT) is known as one of the Sulfa drugs or sulfonamides (SAs), could be existed in the soil, surface water, drinking water and groundwater, whereas, it could be detected in a concentration range of 1.2–4.8% [8,9]. Moreover, SMT like most of SAs is exhibited with poor solubility, therefore, some researching reports were interested in investigating the accessibility to improve the degree of drug solubility via complex formation between SMT and β-cyclodextrin [10–13]. Despite of drug carriers such as β-cyclodextrin is not ascribed as contaminants, but its effects on the absorptivity of risky contaminants must be considerably taken in account [14].

The investigation of polymer quantum dots (PQDs) has extensively considered with the researchers owing to their unique characters such as excellent photoluminescent properties [15], abundancy [16], antimicrobial/anticancer [17], easy of surface decoration for various applications [18], biocompatibility with low toxic effects [19]. PDs are type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a size average less than 10 nm [20]. Owing to their unique characters, they were highly applicable in bioimaging [18], drug delivery [21], photocatalytic application [22], electrocatalytic purpose [23], photovoltaic purposes [24], energy conversion/storing [25], and as sensors [26,27]. Furthermore, carbon containing material composites were recently applied in pharmaceutical degradation and photocatalysis [28–31]. The photoluminescent properties (PL) of PDs demonstrate obvious pathways for sensing purposes. Particularly, well-known processing in fluorescence such as energy transferring, fluorescent quenching, and the sensitivity of fluorescent spectrum for the molecular precursors, all pointing to the wide-scaled applications of PDs in biosensing and chemical sensing [19]. There are two functional moieties for the sensor device, a recognizing element for a specific binding for target analyte and a transducing moiety for signal the reaction [32].

On the other hand, Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) were ascribed as a considerable category of porous crystalline materials with high porosity, in which metal are chemically bonded with organic ligands [33]. Till now, MOFs are interestingly exploited in various purposes such as drug delivery [34], gas storage [35], adsorption/separation [36], metal detection [37], fluorescent sensing [38] photocatalyst & hydrogen evolution [39,40] and water treatment [41].

Herein, a simple and environmentally benign technique was reported to reuse acrylate waste fibers (AWF) for nucleation of PQDs. The so-nucleated PQDs were successively doped within MOF (MIL-125-NH2) to prepare PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as sono-photocatalyst for degradation of sulfamethazine. According to our knowledge, no previously work in literature was considered with approving the affinity of PQDs doped in MOF to prepare PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite for exploitation as sono-photocatalyst. In addition to, clustering of PQDs is significantly considered without requiring of any hazardous materials or solvents, to be compatible for exploitation in sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine as pharmaceutical waste in water stream. Successful ingraining of PQDs by the utilization of AWF under the hydrothermal conditions was confirmed via different instrumental analyses like, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. The affinity of the prepared PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite for sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine was systematically monitored.

Hundred percent acrylic waste fibers (AWF) were collected from the waste of Misr for Spinning and Weaving, Egypt. Titanium isopropoxide (C12H28O5Ti, 97%) 2-aminoterephthalic acid (C8H7NO4, 99%), Methanol (CH4O, 99.9%), N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.8%) and sulfamethazine (99.0%) were supplied Sigma-Aldrich, Germany to be applied as received. whereas, the samples were synthesized by using deionized water.

AWF were firstly washed by nonionic detergent (2 g/L, Hostapal CV from Clariant), at 40 °C for 30 min followed by rinsing with distilled water and dried at ambient temperature. The dried AWF were magnetically stirred in DMF using 1 & 2% (weight/volume) for four hours at ambient temperature. Samples were centrifuged to separate the undissolved particles and the supernatants were transferred to hydrothermal reactor vessel for complete nucleation of PQDs at 135 °C for 5 h. Eventually, the samples were cooled at ambient conditions, to be sequentially centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min (Hermile Z326 K centrifuge) to be separated as insoluble particles. For obtaining the ultra-filtered smaller PQDs, sample were successfully dialyzed by using the 6–8 kDa dialysis kits (Sigma-Adrich).

MIL-125-NH2 was clustered via the reported method in literature [42,43]. Briefly, in methanol/DMF (1:2 v/v) and 3.38 mM of titanium isopropoxide, 2-aminoterephthalic acid (5.5 mM) was dissolved under stirring, whereas, complete clustering of MIL-125-NH2 was under hydrothermal conditions at 150 °C. After one day, the produced precipitate (yellowish in color) was filtrated. by DMF and methanol, the produced precipitate was washed in order to remove the unreacted particles, to be dried under vacuum. Afterwards, under ultrasonic conditions, 0.5 g of MIL-125-NH2 was dispersed in 50 mL of PQDs solution for obtaining PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 for half hour, then, centrifuged to collect PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, washed (by methanol) and lastly, dried under vacuum to be ready for further characterization.

Absorption spectra for AWF, the ingrained PQDs and PODs@MIL-125-NH2 were characterized by using Cary 100 UV–VIS spectrophotometry at wavelength ranged in 200–750 nm. Topography and size distribution of the clustered PQDs were manifested by JEOL-JEM-1200 TEM (Japan). The 4 pi analysis software was used to estimate the size distribution of PQDs. Infrared spectra were obtained via Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometry. The absorption spectra were collected in the wavenumber range of 400 – 4000 cm–1 and the spectra were smoothed by 15 points. The 1HNMR and 13CNMR (nuclear magnetic resonance) spectra were performed on Jeol‐Ex‐300 NMR spectrometry (Japan). Photoluminescence for PQDs was detected, whereas, the spectra were estimated using Spectro-fluorometer (JASCO FP8300). The excitation (at 340 nm) and emission spectra were estimated at room temperature. The diffraction for AWF, the ingrained PQDs and PODs@MIL-125-NH2 were detected λ = 1.5406 Å by using the X'Pert PRO PANalytical diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical).

For achieving the reaction equilibrium, in 10 mL of 1 mg/L sulfamethazine solution, 10 mg of the currently prepared PODs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as dose was dispersed, to be magnetically stirred in the dark environment for 40 minutes. At 25 ± 2 °C, the ultrasonication (ultrasonicator, 40 kHz) was performed in the visible light. Different doses (2 – 18 mg) from PODs@MIL-125-NH2 were used to study the effect of catalyst dosage on sulfamethazine degradation. In order to eliminate the residual catalyst, drug solution was withdrawn after 10 minutes to be centrifuged (10,000 rpm). The centrifuged samples were analysed with UV–Visible spectrophotometry. Afterwards, PODs@MIL-125-NH2 composite was recycled via centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 10 minutes). The recyclable PODs@MIL-125-NH2 composite was 3 times washed by water, ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 120 min. Subsequently PODs@MIL-125-NH2 composite could be re-exploited for the next cycle.

Adsorption and degradation experimental works were varied out three times, the average values were considered and standard error/error bars are presented in all data & results. All kinetics parameters, fitting analysis (R2 & x2) and standard deviations were all estimated by using the origin 8 program. All of the presented data & figures were figured out by origin 8 program.

AWF were successively exploited for nucleation of PQDs under the hydrothermal. The reaction mechanism for clustering of PQDs from textiles wastes could be suggested as follows: firstly, AWF macromolecules are predicted to be hydrolyzed and fragmented, for consequent nucleation of PQDs via re-polymerization, aromatization and eventual oxidation under hydrothermal conditions [44]. Dialysis was performed for ultra-purification of the desired PQDs to obtain mono-dispersed/highly purified PQDs with regulated topography [45]. The altering of the color for sample solutions of PQDs was changed from white to yellow colored solution, whilst, it eventually turned to faint yellow after dialysis.

UV–Visible spectra outcomes for the obtained samples of PQDs that clustered through utilizing of AWF with 2 concentrations (1% & 2%) were presented in Fig. 1 a. While Fig. 1a, declared the spectral data of PQDs which clustered under the hydrothermal conditions. The spectral data showed that, PQDs colloids of AWF were characterized with weak absorption band at 288 nm for π - π* transition (signing for the conjugation in aromatic nucleus) and the relaxation of electron in a broadened band at 372 nm characterized for the transition in n- π* (for the decorative surface groups that might be oxygen containing functional groups) [45,46]. Duplication of AWF concentration resulted in slight intense bands especially for that is characteristic for n - π* transition. These could show that, the prepared PDs could be composed of aromatic conjugated nature that reflect the effect of superiority of AWF for nucleation of PQDs with aromatic character and decorated with functional groups [45,46]. Photoluminescence of PQDs was investigated as it is well known as a sign for the surface decoration with heteroatom containing functional groups [47]. Wu et al. reported that, the photoluminescence of quantum dots (QDs) are characterized for their surface state with decorative groups on their carbonic building units [48]. The surface state is related to the optically active sites which are composed from synergetic hybridization of functional groups on the surface of carbonic core [47,48].

Fig. 1b represents the image for the samples under UV lamp. The optical characters of the obtained PQDs from AWF wastes were performed through the fluorescence emission. Fig. 2 c showed the spectral data of emission after performing the excitation at 370 nm, 400 nm & 440 nm, in order to show that, the maximum excitation is obviously detected at 370 nm. The plotted spectral data in Fig. 2d showed that the excited PQDs prepared from two different concentrations of AWF wastes (1% & 2%) under hydrothermal conditions after the dialysis step to prove the effect of AWF concentration on the optical character of the synthesized PQDs. Form the figured-out data, it is clarified that, the emission peak in UV–visible range affirmed the composition of PQDs with mobile pi-electrons and the non-bonding electrons on the plenty of aromatic carbon-based building skeleton with surface decorative heteroatoms. The surface decoration is well known to be mainly affecting on the location of absorption bands, that in turn results in the coloration of PQDs colloids to endow their easy colorimetric detection. Additionally, duplication of AWF concentration from 1% up to 2% resulted significant sharpening in the emission for the band detected in the visible range (445 nm, i.e., ranged in the green region) from 7000 to 9500 intensity. The optical characters of PQDs as approved from the above-illustrated data could depict that the hydrothermally ingrained PDs with strong fluorescent efficiency in the visible light which consequently facilitate their application in the sensing purpose.

Fig. 2 represented TEM microscopic images, histograms and size distribution for the prepared PQDs via exploitation of AWF with two different concentrations, to inform about the topography and geometry of the nucleated PQDs. The microscopic images strongly confirmed the affinity of AWF in the nucleation of size regulated PQDs, in addition to approve the vital role of its concentration for nucleation of highly dispersed spherical PQDs. Estimation of the distribution in size declared that, PQDs ingrained from 1% of AWF under hydrothermal conditions exhibited with average size of 1.7 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 2a) with 38% of particles were exhibited with particle size of 5.6 nm (Fig. 2c). While, duplication of AWF concentration resulted in nucleation of PQDs with significant enlargement in the particle size (7.4 ± 2.4 nm) with 30% of particles were exhibited with particle size of 10.1 nm (Fig. 2c), that could be attributed to the steric effects results from higher concentration of the exploited wastes [49] (Fig. 2b). So, it could be summarized that, the seen images in Fig. 2 approved the compatibility of hydrolyzed AWF with low concentration of 1% in synthesis of PQDs under hydrothermal conditions to be more desirable for ingraining of shape/size controllable PQDs with surface decorative groups that were hypothesized to be superiorly exploited for the regulation of smaller sized/well dispersed PQDs

For further confirmation for estimation of particle size, Zetasizer analysis were performed for the nano-colloids of PQDs that were currently prepared from AWF with two different concentrations of 1% & 2%, whereas, the estimated data were plotted in Fig. S1a. from the presented data it could be clarified that, smaller sized PQDs (5.6 nm) were successively nucleated from lower concentration of AWF (1%) compared to that PQDs (10.1 nm) clustered from higher concentration of AWF (2%). On the other hand, zeta electric potential was analyzed for identification of the zeta potential of the prepared nano-colloidal solutions using two different concentrations of AWF (1% & 2%). The Zeta potential is an important parameter for estimation the attraction/repulsion between the particles in a colloidal system. The higher is the dispersed particles, the lower is the measured negative zeta potential value of, i.e., higher is the stabilization of the nano-colloidal solution, i.e., as the dispersed PQDs are protected from agglomeration or aggregation. It could be observed from Fig. S1b, the evaluated zeta potential was negatively charged for both of the two prepared nano-colloids, to be ranged at −6 to −9 mV for nano-colloid prepared from 1% AWF, while, it was detected with higher negative values (1 to −3 mV) in case of nano-colloid prepared from 2% AWF. This confirms that, nano-colloidal solution prepared from lower concentration of AWF (1%) were exhibited with better physical stabilization.

On the other hand, Fig. S2a showed FT-IR spectral data for the native AWF & prepared PQDs from AWF with two different concentrations of 1% & 2% under the hydrothermal conditions. From the plotted spectra, it could be observed that, blank AWF exhibited characteristic spectral bands at 2916/2845 cm−1 for CH2 asymmetric aliphatic, an intense and sharp CN stretching peak near 2239 cm−1, 1729 cm−1 (characteristic stretching, C = O of carboxylic acid), 1444 cm −1 and 1374 cm−1 (scissoring and bending vibrations of CH2 and CHCO), 1226 cm−1 (OH bending of carboxyl group) and 1064 cm−1 (alkoxy CO). However, after exploitation of AWF as PQDs precursor, regardless to the concentration of AWF, all the characteristic bands for AWF were recorded with much lower intensity, and two new peaks were estimated at 1655 and 783cm−1 which are corresponded to the stretching vibration of C = C aromatic ring and sp2 CH bending, respectively. This evidence approved the affinity of AWF in clustering of PQDs decorative with oxygen containing functional groups. NMR spectra data were presented in Fig. S3 for further successive elucidation of reaction mechanism. Fig. S3a represented 13CNMR spectrum of AWF based PQDs, while the typical signals for PQDs were appeared at 30 ppm (assigning for sp3 C-), 71 ppm (for COH), 170 ppm (corresponding to C = C in aromatic system or sp2 C) and 181 ppm (referring to C = O). Fig. S3b showed 1HNMR for PQDs synthesized from AWF, while, the spectrum detected the characterized signals for PQDs at 2.5 ppm (sp3 CH), 3–4 ppm (protons of OH), 5.9 ppm (protons of C = O) and at 8.5–8 ppm (protons in sp2 CHCH or aromatic system) [45,50].

UV–Visible spectral results for the synthesized MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 were presented in Fig. 3 a. The MIL-125-NH2 exhibited two absorbance bands at 307 nm (sharp) and 395 nm (broad) which are characterized for π - π* transition and n- π* transition (for oxygen and nitrogen containing groups), respectively. For PQDs@MIL-125-NH2, the spectral band characteristic for n- π* transition was shifted to 404 nm and became more intense due to the formation of composite. This is attributed to the oxygen and nitrogen containing groups of PQDs and MIL-125-NH2 [45,46].

The topography and geometrical features for the MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 was investigated via TEM images as shown in Fig. 3b&3c. The micrographs confirmed that the particles of polymer dots were well distributed over the disk shaped MIL-125-NH2 crystals. The size distribution and particle size of the PQDs after doping onto MIL-125-NH2 were slightly higher (4–16 nm and 8.7 nm, respectively) compared to that the individual PQDs. From Fig. S4a, the estimated zeta potential for MIL-125-NH2 was (−18 to −22 mV) negatively charged. While, it was obtained with lower negative values of −11 to −15 mV in case of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite which is logically located in the potential range between the individual materials.

The optical band gaps (Eg) for MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 were evaluated by using UV-diffuse reflectance spectra and Tauc equation, while the obtained spectra were presented in Fig. S4b. The estimated band gap was lowered from 2.58 eV for MIL-125-NH2 to 2.14 eV for PQDs@MIL-125-NH2. The decrement in band gap value in case of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite indicates to its photocatalytic activity in the sunlight.

The diffraction of X-ray results for the synthesized PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 were displayed in Fig. S4. MIL-125-NH2 exhibited five main diffractions at 2θ° = 6.6°, 9.5°, 11.4°, 17.6° and 19.2° which are characteristic for the crystalline structure (101, 022, 211, 222 and 004) of MIL-125-NH2 [43,51,52]. The synthesized PQDs showed one broad diffraction peak at 17.1° and five sharp diffractions at 2θ° = 24.9°, 37.5°, 43.9°, 64.3° and 77.4° After doping step, the characteristic diffractions of PQDs were appeared with low intense besides that of MIL-125-NH2 which affirmed the formation of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2.

Pharmaceutical pollutants in water streams with could be ascribed as one of the most toxic water pollutants. Functionalization of nano-objects for degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants (such as antiviral drugs, antibiotics, hormones…etc..) in the water stream was interestingly attracted the researchers' attention during the last decade. The nano-catalyst is uniquely characterized with a large surface area, environment friendly, high adsorption capacity, and have a high accessibility for the inorganic and organic compounds. Polymer quantum dots as a type of carbon quantum dots is known to be composed of carbon atoms in the main skeleton and decorated with heteroatom containing functional groups with electron affinity, flexibility, and mechanical strength [53], therefore they could be beneficially exploited useful for treatment of water and desalination [54]. The most applicable absorbents for wastewater treatment are the activated carbon, owing to their broad-scaled removal affinity, less expensiveness, high absorptivity, and easy of disposal. But, the as-mentioned types of adsorbents are reported to be disadvantageous with slow adsorptive kinetics and difficult of recycling [55].

For removing of pollutants or contaminants from water, the chemical degradation is considered as one from the most preferable techniques as mentioned in the previous reports [56–60]. Recently, sonolysis was interestingly considered as an advanced oxidation process (AOP) for the degradation of different water contaminants [61–64]. Comparing with the other wastewater treatment techniques, the chemical degradation is described as low efficacy energy consuming technique. Therefore, to improve its affinity, different co-operative agents were been added in the sonolysis [65,66]. Till now, no researches were concerned with the application of PDs doped in MIL-125-NH2 composites as catalyst for improving the sonolysis affinity. Additionally, no researching reports were studied the exploitation of MOF containing PQDs as recoverable sono-photocatalyst for degradation of pharmaceutical wastes from water effluents. Herein, the synthesized PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, as a removable catalyst, was applied for the sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine (pharmaceutical wastes).

Fig. S5 showed the effect of exploiting PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite on the removal percentage% and degradation amounts (mg/g) for sono-catalytic degradation of sulfamethazine. From the plotted results, after 90 min, without catalyst, the removal percentage and degradation amounts were estimated to be 22% and 54 mg/g, respectively. Whereas, by exploitation of PQDs as sono-catalyst resulted in detection of the removal percentage and degradation amounts with values of 32% and 83 mg/g, respectively. Whereas, the highest removal percentage (63%) and degradation amounts (158 mg/g) were estimated by applying of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite.

In the case of sono-photocatalytic degradation, in addition to the previously illustrated effect of sonolysis in degradation of water pollutants, irradiation that exhibited with photon energy much higher than the band gap of PQDs results in generation of hole pairs and electron, that in turn helping in the liberation of much higher amounts of ROS [19–23] to decompose water molecules and produce much higher amounts of ·OH, ·H, and ·O radicals to oxidize and degrade much higher amounts of the water pollutants [26]. Firstly, the effect of catalyst dosage was study as shown in Fig. S6. The sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine was gradually increased by increasing the dose of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite from 2 mg to 10 mg owing to the enlargement in number of active species. Further increment in doses of catalyst was accompanied with decrement in sulfamethazine degradation which could be explained by the high turbidity in the suspension caused high light scattering effect and the penetration of light irradiation inside degradation medium became low. Therefore, the optimal dose of catalyst is 10 mg which will apply in the kinetic of sulfamethazine degradation under sono-photocatalytic.

Fig. 4 represents the effect of sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine via exploiting PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite on degradation percentage and degradation amounts, and the figured out data showed that, the lowest degradation percentage (32%) and degradation amounts (76 mg/g) were estimated in case of absence of catalyst. However, degradation percentage & degradation amounts were observably increased in case of using MIL-125-NH2 to be 73% & 188 mg/g, respectively. Full degradation (99.9%) and the highest degradation amounts (244 mg/g) were estimated in case of applying PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as sono-photocatalyst for degradation of sulfamethazine.

Comparative overview between the estimated data in Figs. S5 & 4, it could be depicted that, effect of irradiation reflected in enhancement the sonolysis degradation of sulfamethazine. It is greatly maximized in case of exploiting PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite under the effect of photo-sonolysis for sulfamethazine (100% removal percentage) (Fig. 4). Under the effect of sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine was shown to follow the order of PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite > MIL-125-NH2 > PQDs. The kinetic parameters for degradation of sulfamethazine in aquatic stream were estimated for first ordered reaction. The concept for the first order is hypothesized as that the degradation rate of sulfamethazine is mainly dependent on the currently prepared sono-photocatalyst. Qe (the experimentally adsorption), first order rate constant (k1), coefficient of determination (R2) and Chi square (x2) as one from the most applied fitting analysis, [67,68] were all evaluated for the sono-catalytic and sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine using PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 & PQDs@MIL-125-NH2.

From the estimated results in Table 1 , sulfamethazine as one of the pharmaceutical wastes in aquatic streams were sono-catalytically degraded with and without the effect of irradiation i.e., via sono-catalysis and sono-photocatalysis in order to show the effect of irradiation in enhancement the affinity of the prepared reagents for degradation of more amounts from the tested pollutant. For sono-catalytic degradation, constant of K1 values were estimated to be 26.06 × 10−3 min−1 after exploitation of PQDs. Whereas, it was significantly increased to 32.22 × 10−3 using MIL-125-NH2, to be maximized up to 39.78 × 10−3 min−1 for PQDs@MIL-125-NH2. Moreover, the degradation rate for sulfamethazine was speeded up by factor of 1.5, 1.9 and 2.3 using PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2, respectively. These can approve the catalytic potentiality of the ingrained PQDs within the prepared composite.

Similarly, for sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine, Table 1 also declared that, K1 for sulfamethazine degradation was grown from 21.45 × 10−3 min−1 using PQDs to 24.90 × 10−3 min−1 for MIL-125-NH2, to be maximized up to 28.79 × 10−3 min−1 for PQDs@MIL-125-NH2. Additionally, the rate of sulfamethazine degradation was speeded up by factors of 2.0, 2.4 and 2.7 using PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2, respectively. eventually by comparing between the estimated values for degradation of sulfamethazine under sono-catalysis and sono-photocatalysis, it could be approved that, processing the reaction under the irradiation effect was accompanied with significant acceleration of the rate of sulfamethazine sono-catalytic degradation. Immobilization of PQDs showed superiority in catalytic potentiality for the synthesized PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite, that is in agreement with previous reports [69,70].

Table 2 represents a summary for a comparative study to show the superiority of the prepared PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite in sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine. From the tabulated data it could be declared that, except GO@Bi [71] that takes 120 min for full degradation, PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite exhibited excellence catalytic action for full degradation (99.9%) of sulfamethazine after 90 min, rather than Ce doped Fe (74%) [72], Fe doped TiO2 (74.8%) [73], Fe3O4 (80%) [74], Fe-Cu/sulfite (87%) [75], Fe3O4/Mn3O4 (92%) [76], Fe3O4@activated carbon (94.7) [74], P doped TiO2 (97%) [77] and PdNPs@AuNPs@MCC (99%) [78].

The potency of any applicable son-photocatalyst to be reused is essentially required to be affirmed for wide-scaled applicability especially in the environmental field. Therefore, the synthesized PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as sono-photocatalyst was monitored for their recoverability to sulfamethazine degradation as one of the pharmaceutical wastes in aquatic streams. After the first cycle, the synthesized PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite may be isolated from sulfamethazine solution by centrifugation to be washed up with distilled water for leaching out the degraded moieties followed by washing up with ethanol and dried before its usage in the higher cycle. The procedure of recycling was repeated for 5 five consecutive cycles and the evaluated data are figured out in Fig. 5 a. The sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine was marginally lowered after recycling. After 5 cycles, the degraded percentages of sulfamethazine were lowered from 63%, 72% and 99.9% to 53%, 67% and 92% by exploiting of PQDs, MIL-125-NH2 and PQDs@MIL-125-NH2, respectively. Eventually, the analyzed data could approve the role of PQDs in enhancing the recyclability of MIL-125-NH2 to produce PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 with superior recyclability for five repeated cycles. Whereas, the degradation of the tested sulfamethazine was reduced by only 7.9% after five cycles, to reflect the recoverability of the prepared PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 as a catalyst for sono-photocatalytic degradation, to be applied for many treatment cycles.

Weight of the PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 catalyst was carefully measured after the degradation cycles for checking the mass loss. The weight of catalyst was marginally lowered from 10 mg/g to 8.6 mg/g after five reusing cycles. The mass loss in PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 catalyst was 14% within five cycles by means it was 2.8% only in each cycle which is negligible by considering the regeneration process. This means that the PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 catalyst or its ingredients were not almost leached during the photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine.

The XRD for PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 catalyst was detected after the sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine to check the stability of catalyst after application. The recorded results in Fig. S7 notified that the fundamental diffraction peaks for catalyst was not insignificantly changed after degradation which proved the substantial stability against application.

The mechanism for degradation of sulfamethazine under the effect of the prepared PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 could be suggested as schematic in Fig. 5b & 5c. Briefly, in the absence of ultrasound irradiation, MIL-125-NH2 as a supporting powder is suggested to act in removing of the pharmaceutical waste via its adsorptive surface, while, under sonolysis, the ultrasound waves results in water decomposition for liberation of the highly active species of free radicals including O2 ., HO., and HOO., which are subsequently acted in decomposition of the sulfamethazine to produce water and carbon dioxide [79,80]. However, proceeding the sono-catalysis under the effect of irradiation, i.e., sono-photocatalytic degradation, resulted in increment of the efficiency for sonolysis degradation of sulfamethazine via increment of the amount of the liberated active radical species with more vigorous degradation of water molecules for degradation of more molecules of sulfamethazine. Moreover, ingraining of PQDs within MIL-125-NH2 with ultrasonication and under the effect of irradiation increases the affinity for the degradation of sulfamethazine, attributing to their catalytic potentiality, which may significantly speed up the degradation rate as approved form the above-explained data. Meanwhile, PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 exhibited the maximum affinity for degradation of the tested pollutant that is logically corresponded to the exploitation of active catalytic surface for both of ingrained PQDs & MIL-125-NH2 [81]. The PQDs could be used as collectors and transporters for the electrons, and consequently the holes and electrons could act as reactive centres for forming higher reactive species to degrade sulfamethazine [82,83].

Therefore, the effect of different quenchers was performed to specify the type of effective reactive centers in the sono-photodegradation of sulfamethazine (Fig. 5b). The sono-photolysis degradation percentage of sulfamethazine was measured in presence of ammonium oxalate and isopropyl alcohol quenchers as scavenging agent for holes (h +) and hydroxyl radicals (OH•), respectively. The degradation percentage of sulfamethazine was significantly reduced to 51% & 74% at using ammonium oxalate and isopropyl alcohol, respectively. The obtained data reflected the important role of holes and hydroxyl radicals in the sono-photo degradation of sulfamethazine. Subsequently, under ultrasonic waves, light irradiation resulted in electron excitation from the valence band (VB) of MIL-125-NH2 to conduction band (CB) forming holes in the VB. The excited electrons are transport to PQDs and consequently reacted with O2 forming superoxide anion (O2 .−) which helped in forming HOO. followed by OH. radicals. While, the generated holes in VB is quite positive enough in order to absorb the negative species of OH− to produce OH. Which responsible for sulfamethazine degradation (Fig. 5c). So, it could be summarized that, the innovated method for degradation of the studied sulfamethazine using the as-prepared PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite resulted in superior improvement for the application of sono-photocatalytic degradation, reflecting in a positive techno-economic assessment.

Numerous approaches focused on the effect of Sulfonamides or sulfa drugs (SAs) on the environmental pollution, were looked in details at the possible risky effects of pollution. Herein, a simple and environmentally benign technique was reported to reuse AWF for nucleation of PQDs with average size of 5.6 nm. The so-nucleated PQDs were successively doped within MIL-125-NH2 to prepare PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite as sono-photocatalyst for degradation of sulfamethazine. Clustering of PQDs was carried out without requiring of any hazardous compounds or solvents, to be compatible for exploitation in sono-photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethazine. The highest degradation rate was obtained for PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 in case of sono-photocatalysis, as sulfamethazine was almost degraded (99.9%) in 90 min with degradation amount of 244 mg/g. the degradation percentage of sulfamethazine was insignificantly diminished to 96% after reusing for 5 cycles. The obtained findings suggested that the synthesized PQDs@MIL-125-NH2 composite could be successfully applied in removal of sulfamethazine containing drugs & pharmaceutical intermediates in specific or in water contamination removal in general.
